titleStmt.title: GraphSeek: Next-Generation Graph Analytics with LLMs

analytic.title: GraphSeek: Next-Generation Graph Analytics with LLMs

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents
application.label: a91ee48

startPage=-1, endPage=-1, consolidateCitations=0, consolidateHeader=0, consolidateFunders=0, includeRawAffiliations=false, includeRawCitations=false, includeRawCopyrights=false, generateTeiIds=false, generateTeiCoordinates=[], sentenceSegmentation=false, flavor=null

div.p: Graphs are foundational across domains but remain hard to use without deep expertise. LLMs promise accessible natural language (NL) graph analytics, yet they fail to process industry-scale property graphs effectively and efficiently: such datasets are large, highly heterogeneous, structurally complex, and evolve dynamically. To address this, we devise a novel abstraction for complex multi-query analytics over such graphs. Its key idea is to replace brittle generation of graph queries directly from NL with planning over a Semantic Catalog that describes both the graph schema and the graph operations. Concretely, this induces a clean separation between a Semantic Plane for LLM planning and broader reasoning, and an Execution Plane for deterministic, database-grade query execution over the full dataset and tool implementations. This design yields substantial gains in both token efficiency and task effectiveness even with small-context LLMs. We use this abstraction as the basis of the first LLM-enhanced graph analytics framework called GraphSeek. GraphSeek achieves substantially higher success rates (e.g., 86% over enhanced LangChain) and points toward the next generation of affordable and accessible graph analytics that unify LLM reasoning with database-grade execution over large and complex property graphs.

div.head: INTRODUCTION
div.p: Graphs are critically important in academia and industry in numerous domains, including bioinformatics (e.g., analyzing protein structures), social sciences (e.g., studying human interactions), chemistry (e.g., designing chemical compounds), medicine (e.g., discovering drugs), cybersecurity (e.g., identifying intruders), healthcare (e.g., exposing fraudulent claims), web graph analysis (e.g., fostering accurate search services), and many others [27,119,124]. For example, graph databases are more powerful for representing and querying complex relationships than relational and document databases [6,67,81,90,107]. Graph query languages like Cypher [78] and Gremlin [155] as well as graph databases and frameworks such as Kùzu [77] and GDI [25] enable effective pattern matching [5,154], with linear time traversal performance compared to costly relational joins, fostering practical solutions [154]. Beyond graph databases, graph analytic frameworks and schemes such as GraphMineSuite [42] or ProbGraph [36] have also demonstrated their ability to capture patterns and make predictions that would be impossible or less accurate using traditional flat data analytics [23,157,159]. Yet, graphs impose high learning curve as they usually demand skill in graph theory, graph query languages, and advanced modeling decisions.

Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a promising opportunity to make graph analytics more accessible by enabling interaction through natural language, without requiring deep technical expertise in graph theory or query languages [16,135,205]. However, realizing this vision at industry scale remains challenging. Most LLMs are trained primarily on natural language (NL) and code and thus struggle with precise graph pattern matching, schema-aware query formulation, and multi-hop traversal. This gap is amplified in realworld settings, where industrial graphs are highly heterogeneous and governed by evolving schemas. A seemingly straightforward strategy based on injecting graph structure into the prompt is infeasible as production graphs can span millions of nodes and edges. Practical systems must also support database-grade operations including filtering, aggregation, temporal constraints, transactional updates, and structured result presentation. The grand challenge is to enable NL-driven graph analytics that is both effective and efficient under tight token and latency budgets.

We address this challenge by introducing a graph processing abstraction for large and complex heterogeneous property graphs (contribution 1). Its key component is a Semantic Catalog , a compact semantic interface that supports the Semantic Plane, where an LLM agent plans and interprets analytics requests (top of Figure 1). The catalog is motivated by a practical observation: directly translating NL into backend graph queries is brittle and often misaligned with complex graph schemas and graph access patterns. It makes both the graph data and the graph operations legible to an LLM without fine-tuning or long-context prompting. It contains (a) Schema Descriptors, concise NL annotations for schema elements that ground domain terminology and reduce ambiguity even for specialized in-house schemas, and (b) Operator Descriptors, a list of intermediate graph operators (e.g., 𝑘-hop traversal, temporal slicing, neighborhood aggregation) with explicit NL annotations that clarify operator behavior with respect to the graph data. Together, these components replace brittle generation of graph queries from NL with robust planning over a Semantic Catalog.

To turn catalog-grounded plans into efficient graph analytics, we also require an explicit execution model that bridges Semantic and Execution Planes (Figure 1, bottom). For this, we harness TAG (Table-Augmented Generation) [47], a recent execution model for relational databases augmented with LLMs, which we extend to graph processing and multi-step queries (contribution 2). TAG decomposes NL questions into query synthesis S , query execu- tion E , and answer generation G . In our instantiation, synthe- sis and generation operate in the Semantic Plane: synthesis maps NL to typed operator invocations drawn from the Semantic Catalog, and generation produces answers from compact intermediate views. Execution operates in the Execution Plane: a Non-LLM runtime deterministically compiles and runs these invocations on the graph backend (minimizing token costs). We further add a Decision stage D to make the loop multi-step, enabling token-bounded re- finement and self-correction: intermediate results are materialized out-of-context and referenced across steps, while only compact 𝑂 (1) summaries and selected subsets are returned in-context.

We implement this abstraction in GraphSeek, the first LLMenhanced system for interactive processing of production-scale property graphs, and we detail its design (contribution 3). Using GraphSeek, we develop and detail five real-world industry use cases drawn from an electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing setting, illustrating the end-to-end workflow and the kinds of multi-query analytics enabled by our Semantic/Execution Plane design (contribution 4). We compare GraphSeek against established LLM-based graph analytics baselines (contribution 5). Across diverse workloads, Graph-Seek consistently achieves higher success rates (86% over an enhanced LangChain [109]) while maintaining low token costs and runtimes. Ablation studies isolate the effect of the Semantic Catalog, showing substantial gains in reliability and query correctness. Overall, GraphSeek demonstrates how to unify LLM reasoning with database-grade execution for graph analytics, and provides an operational blueprint for multi-hop TAG-style systems over large-scale heterogeneous property graphs.

div.head: LANDSCAPE OF GRAPHS & LLMS
div.p: No existing graph-oriented scheme enables interactive graph analytics over industry-scale graphs without demanding expertise in graph query/theory. Existing approaches either assume small, incontext graphs, focus on predictive representation learning rather than symbolic query execution, or are built for non-graph backends, and thus fundamentally cannot support precise, multi-hop, and schema-aware queries.

First, several works have explored the intersection of graphs and LLMs, but focused on simple prompting or fine-tuning to perform basic algorithmic tasks (e.g., graph connectivity) or learning tasks (e.g., node classification) on small graphs that fit entirely in the model's context [49,61,112,114,116,145,162,178]. Some studies have also addressed the generation of graph queries in languages such as SPARQL [92], emphasizing the syntactic and semantic correctness of query generation [10,51,75,111,131,136,150,161]. However, none of these schemes considers the broader scope of industry-scale graph analytics. These approaches assume that graph structure or large schema fragments can be serialized into an LLM context and that the model can emit correct free-form queries; yet,

div.head: GRAPH PROCESSING ABSTRACTION
div.p: We introduce a graph processing abstraction that makes NL-based analytics over property graphs both robust and scalable. The abstraction has two tightly coupled components: (i) an execution model that extends TAG [47] to multi-step graph analytics with self-correction, and (ii) a Semantic Catalog that serves as a compact interface between language and the graph backend. The model retains TAG's three conceptual stages (query synthesis, query execution, and answer generation) but makes two changes essential for graph analytics: (1) synthesis produces typed operator invocations rather than free-form backend queries, and (2) the pipeline is iterative, producing and consuming intermediate results across multiple steps. Together, they replace brittle direct graph-query generation from NL with catalog-grounded planning in the Semantic Plane, while delegating all query execution to the Execution Plane with exact database semantics.

div.head: Execution Model
div.p: We model the data source as a labeled property graph (LPG) 𝐺 = (𝑉 , 𝐸, 𝜆 𝑉 , 𝜆 𝐸 , 𝜋), where 𝑉 and 𝐸 are nodes and edges, 𝜆 𝑉 (𝑣) and 𝜆 𝐸 (𝑒) map to (possibly multiple) labels/types, and 𝜋 maps nodes/edges to key-value properties. The schema S specifies allowed node/edge types, property keys and value domains, and relationship signatures. A graph backend exposes an executable query language (e.g., Cypher) and a transactional runtime.

Let L be a library of typed semantic operators. Each operator 𝑙 ∈ L has a compilation procedure into one or more backend queries. Operators are not backend queries themselves; they form an intermediate representation designed for controllable synthesis. Operators in the catalog fall into four groups: graph data operators that issue queries against the source graph database and materialize results; retrieval operators that operate over previously materialized results and, when needed, upload larger result chunks into the LLM context; presentation operators that create or refine visualizations of prior tabular or graph outputs; and agent operators that invoke an LLM agent with an isolated context to perform specific sub-tasks. Given a natural language request 𝑅 over 𝐺, the system maintains a step-indexed interaction state
div.formula: 𝜎 𝑡 = (𝑅, 𝐺, S, L, C, A 𝑡 , T 𝑡 ),
div.p: where 𝑡 is the current step, C is the Semantic Catalog, A 𝑡 is an artifact store of executor-produced intermediate results represented by handles (node/edge sets, projected tables, aggregate summaries, subgraphs), and T 𝑡 is a compact execution trace (messages, operator calls, and outcomes) up to step 𝑡. This state makes explicit the multi-step contribution: the system composes dependent operator calls across steps and can revise earlier choices based on execution feedback.
Each step 𝑡 decomposes into four stages:
div.formula: Synthesis (Stage 1) S : syn 𝑡 (𝜎 𝑡 ; C) → 𝑎 𝑡 (1)
div.p: Execution (Stage 2) E : exec(𝑎 𝑡 , 𝐺 ) → 𝑟 𝑡
div.formula: Generation (Stage 3) G : gen 𝑡 (𝑅, T 𝑡 , 𝑟 𝑡 ; C) → 𝑦 𝑡(3)
div.p: Decision (Stage 4) D : dec 𝑡 (𝑦 𝑡 , 𝑟 𝑡 , T 𝑡 ) → {halt, continue} (4) where 𝑎 𝑡 is a typed operator invocation 𝑎 𝑡 = ⟨𝑜 𝑡 , 𝜃 𝑡 ⟩ with operator 𝑜 𝑡 ∈ L and arguments 𝜃 𝑡 , 𝑟 𝑡 is the executor response, and 𝑦 𝑡 is a candidate answer that may be final or may indicate the need for additional evidence (e.g., "need another traversal to resolve ambiguity"). Crucially, syn and gen are catalog conditioned: in synthesis, the LLM selects and instantiates operators, and interprets returned artifacts, relying on Schema and Operator Descriptors. The Non-LLM Executor deterministically compiles 𝑎 𝑡 into one or more backend queries (e.g., Cypher), executes them over 𝐺, and returns a full result object persisted out-of-context as a handle in A 𝑡 +1 , and a compact in-context observation (small samples, aggregates, statistical summaries) optionally augmented with Artifact Descriptors from C. This design keeps the LLM context 𝑂 (1) with respect to |𝑉 | + |𝐸| while retaining exact database semantics.

The loop continues while dec 𝑡 returns continue and stops when it returns halt (emitting 𝑦 𝑡 as the final answer) or a step budget is reached. Decision enables token-bounded self-correction: if 𝑟 𝑡 indicates an issue (incorrect operator choice, empty results, etc.) then dec 𝑡 selects continue, and syn revises 𝑎 𝑡 +1 based on S, C, and the trace T 𝑡 ; otherwise it returns the answer.

div.head: Semantic Catalog
div.p: The Semantic Catalog C is a compact, queryable interface that grounds both what the data means and what computations mean. We use the term Semantic Descriptor to denote a short NL annotation attached to an object, and distinguish two main descriptor families: Schema Descriptors and Operator Descriptors. Both are consumed by the execution model: syn uses Schema/Operator Descriptors to map 𝑅 to 𝑎 𝑡 , and gen uses Schema/Artifact Descriptors to verbalize 𝑟 𝑡 and to decide whether to continue.

While not required for correctness, the Semantic Catalog improves disambiguation of symbolic structures, aligns user intent with schema elements, and supports interpretation of complex outputs. We use it to augment standard property-graph schemas into semantic graph schemas, enriching node types, relationship types, and attributes with domain-specific natural language descriptors that capture intent and context. These descriptors are authored by experts and/or refined by LLMs, and encode proprietary, companyspecific semantics that no general model can reliably infer a priori. In our setting, their footprint is small (about 2k tokens for the entire graph). GraphSeek exposes an API to inject such knowledge (e.g., business glossaries, process labels, codebooks). To reduce authoring cost and variability, we use a hybrid pipeline: drafts are produced from schema introspection (types, constraints), sample values, existing documentation, and prior query logs; LLMs propose candidates under strict schema-grounded templates, and humans validate them before integration.

div.head: GRAPHSEEK: DESIGN & ARCHITECTURE
div.p: We now overview GraphSeek (Figure 3) that implements our abstraction. GraphSeek comprises three cooperating modules: the Controller, the Hybrid Data Store, and the Adaptive Toolset. The Controller contains an LLM Agent (Semantic Plane) and a Non-LLM Executor (Execution Plane). The agent interprets user intent and instantiates operator invocations from the Semantic Catalog; these invocations are realized via tools from the Adaptive Toolset and are compiled and executed against the graph backend by the Non-LLM Executor. Results flow into the Hybrid Data Store. The Controller can also propose edits to tool definitions, which are implemented after validation and optional human approval.

div.head: Controller & Token-Efficient Data Reasoning
div.p: To minimize token costs & latency and maximize effectiveness, the LLM Agent never operates on raw graph content nor emits free-form Cypher. Instead, given a user request 𝑥 and the Semantic Catalog C, it produces a typed operator call 𝑙 (𝜃 ), where each 𝑙 ∈ L is an operator annotated in C and 𝜃 are schema-validated arguments. Operators are implemented by tools: each operator call 𝑙 (𝜃 ) maps to a concrete tool invocation 𝑡 = 𝜏 (𝑙, 𝜃 ) in the Adaptive Toolset, whose full code lives in the Execution Plane. The Non-LLM Executor deterministically compiles each tool invocation into backend queries (e.g., Cypher), executes them, post-processes results, and returns only compact 𝑂 (1) summaries 𝑠 𝑡 = 𝜎 (𝑟 𝑡 ) to the agent for the next step 𝑡. Full payloads 𝑟 𝑡 are stored out-of-context and retrieved only on demand (e.g., via retrieval tools) when additional detail is required. This architecture separates the LLM reasoning in the Semantic Plane and the Non-LLM execution in the Execution Plane. We present an example compact graph summary in Appendix A.

div.head: Hybrid Data Store
div.p: The Hybrid Data Store provides persistent state across the two planes. First, a graph database stores the input graph 𝐺. Second, all tool outputs are written to a Results Data Cache. Only compact summaries 𝑠 = 𝜎 (𝑟 ) are returned in-context; full payloads 𝑟 (tables, graph fragments, files) can be retrieved on demand. Summaries may include tabular outputs (schema/column metadata, head/tail previews, etc.), textual outputs (truncated bodies with key metadata), and graph outputs (node/edge counts, small visualizable subgraphs, etc.). Third, a message history preserves conversational context for consistent multi-step reasoning. Finally, a task store records task definitions, execution traces, and logs for inspection and reuse. Each task trace captures the chain (𝑥 → 𝜋 → {𝑡 𝑡 } → {𝑟 𝑡 } → {𝑠 𝑡 } → 𝑦), enabling end-to-end visualization and fine-grained debugging/auditing. Traces and conversations are interactive artifacts: users can revisit any step to branch, refine, or replay segments.

div.head: Adaptive Toolset
div.p: The Adaptive Toolset supplies the Execution Plane with the concrete tool implementations that realize Catalog Operators. Tools are schema-validated, self-documenting function specifications that the LLM instantiates (via operator calls) and the Non-LLM Executor deterministically compiles into precise backend actions. The library covers routines for graph access (entity lookups, neighborhood and multi-hop traversals, aggregations, temporal slicing, etc.), result retrieval/manipulation (loading full outputs or slices, conversions between graph and tables, constrained dataframe operations, joins/merges, etc.), presentation (tables, charts, etc.), and agent utilities (isolated-context subtasks, controlled fallbacks). The toolset is extensible. Critically, it is also self-adaptive: at inference time the LLM may propose edits to a tool; the system validates candidates against contracts (e.g., JSON Schema), compiles and dry-runs against the live schema, versions the candidate, and (optionally) gates promotion via human approval.

div.head: Details of System Workflow
div.p: The execution of analytics tasks in GraphSeek is detailed in Figure 3 (center). It begins when the user submits a natural language request 𝑥 via the chat interface 1 . In the Semantic Plane, the LLM Agent processes 𝑥 under a contextual prompt composed of the system instruction, the Semantic Catalog C, prior interaction history, and a structured list of callable tools.

The LLM responds with either a plain-text message 𝑦 or a structured operator invocation (mapped to a tool call) 2 . In the latter case 3 , the system forwards the call to the Non-LLM Executor (Execution Plane), which executes the specified tool with arguments 4 . Tool logic may query the source graph database, operate over previously materialized intermediate results referenced by IDs, or run custom computation logic.

The output of tool execution 5 (typically a graph fragment or structured table) is stored in the Result Data Cache as one or more objects (e.g., DataFrames, graph views). To enable coherent multistep reasoning within token limits, a compact natural language summary 𝑠 = 𝜎 (𝑟 ) is generated 6 and appended to the message history passed to the LLM Agent in the next turn 7 . The loop continues until the LLM Agent emits a plain-text response 𝑦 8 , presented to the user along with relevant visualizations (tables, charts, graphs) 9 .

div.head: GRAPHSEEK: EXAMPLE USE CASES
div.p: We provide five use cases of GraphSeek and also pinpoint Graph-Seek's advantages over traditional approaches (due to space constraints, detailed listings of each use case are in Appendix D). We select these use cases to showcase the ability of GraphSeek to automatically detect and execute graph analytics patterns such as aggregation, traversal, graph-level comparison, and fuzzy property lookups. Moreover, they highlight the capability of the system to conduct multi-step reasoning by splitting a complex question into sub-queries, and to accelerate reasoning by identifying parallelization opportunities in tool execution.

div.head: A Running Example: EV Manufacturing
div.p: The use cases harness an anonymized but representative electric vehicle (EV) manufacturing graph

foot_0 , see Figure 1 (bottom). Production is organized into sequential assembly tiers (tier 0 corresponds to a finished vehicle model; higher tiers represent upstream subassemblies and modules). At each tier, an assembly process executed on a specific assembly line within a factory site transforms components (e.g., battery modules and prior-tier drive assemblies) into a new subsystem, potentially generating auxiliary modules or scrap; intermediate subsystems may be transferred across factory sites within a single vehicle's manufacturing flow. The graph links vehicle models, intermediate assemblies, component modules, assembly lines, factory sites, organizational units, and regions, together with temporal annotations (e.g., month/quarter, plan vs. actual) and cost/quantity information. The schema captures key entities and relationships involved in the design, production, and deployment of modular EV components. It defines node types such as BatteryModule (individual energy storage units), DriveAssembly (motor, inverter, and transmission components), VehicleModel (complete EV configurations), FactorySite (physical production facilities), and AssemblyLine (production lines used to build specific component types). Relationships include INTEGRATED_IN, OUTPUTS, INSTALLED_AT, and CON-NECTED_TO. Example relation types could be (:DriveAssembly) -

Each node type includes domain-specific attributes that enable detailed analytics: e.g., BatteryModule nodes may store energy-Density and configVersion; DriveAssembly nodes may include effi-ciencyRate or assemblyCost; and FactorySite nodes may contain operational metadata such as siteCode or region.

div.head: Example Semantic Descriptors
div.p: In the EV graph, for instance, the DriveAssembly node may be annotated as: "Represents a partially assembled propulsion subsystem within a multi-stage vehicle production process; it may consume earlier components (e.g., electric motors) and supply integrated assemblies to final vehicle models. " Likewise, the INTEGRATED_IN relation might be described as: "Denotes that a component or subsystem, such as a battery or drive assembly, is physically and functionally incorporated into a higher-level product during assembly." This semantic overlay allows the LLM to distinguish functionally distinct but structurally similar elements (such as battery packs and drive systems) not merely by label but by their role in the assembly flow.

div.head: Example Use Cases
div.p: We now illustrate GraphSeek on five real-world EV manufacturing requests. Each use case is written as an industry question, but is also representative of (i) a recurring graph access pattern (e.g., filtered selection, neighborhood traversal, aggregation), and (ii) a related data-reasoning feature of our Semantic/Execution Plane design (e.g., catalog grounding, multi-step reasoning, parallel tool calls, self-correction). In all cases, the Semantic Plane compiles the request into a short plan 𝜋 = (𝑜 1 (𝜃 1 ), . . . , 𝑜 𝑇 (𝜃 𝑇 )) of typed operator invocations from the Semantic Catalog, while the Execution Plane deterministically executes corresponding tools on the graph backend and returns compact summaries (with full results cached out-of-context).

Aggregating Upstream Modules per Factory. The first example shows answering a question "How many baseline drive assemblies do we produce per factory?" (Listing 2). This question requires constructing an aggregate view over the graph. The LLM agent correctly interprets "baseline assemblies" as drive assemblies at the initial process tier (processTier = 0), selects an appropriate graph tool, and returns a human-readable summary.

This process stresses the pattern of attribute-filtered node selection followed by group-by aggregation. The data reasoning pattern is schema/terminology grounding via the Semantic Catalog: the agent resolves base-tier/baseline to a typed predicate (assemblyTier = 0), selects the correct node type (DriveAssembly) and grouping key (factoryCode), and emits a typed operator invocation consistent with the catalog signature.

In GraphSeek, the user provides only a high-level, domain oriented NL query. Without GraphSeek, one would need to understand the exact graph schema and write a Cypher query like the one in Listing 2. Such queries must also adapt to differences in semantics or structure across domains. For non-experts, learning schema, properties, and syntax is a significant barrier. In GraphSeek, the LLM handles this mapping and query generation automatically, while the graph engine performs efficient execution.

div.head: Inspecting a Production
div.p: Plan for a Vehicle. Here, a user requests a "unique production plan" for a given vehicle model (Listing 3). The system retrieves, composes, and explains a full subgraph representing the plan. The user expresses a high-level question about the plan for model PX-206, and the system resolves the name to a VehicleModel node, traverses its neighborhood (e.g., Battery-Module, DriveAssembly, AssemblyLine, FactorySite), and returns both a human-readable graph (via graph_handle) and a textual explanation.

The stressed data-reasoning feature is compositional operator planning plus result interpretation: the Semantic Plane resolves the model name, selects a plan-extraction operator, and then explains the returned graph handle using the Schema/Operator Descriptors. The Execution Plane performs the neighborhood traversal and returns a compact structural summary together with a graph_handle that the UI can render. A manual query requires understanding which labels and relationships compose a production plan (e.g., CONSUMED_IN, GEN-ERATES, PROCESSED_AT), and encoding those paths. This often involves iterative refinement to capture cost types, uniqueness constraints, and correct traversal depth. GraphSeek handles all of this with the LLM's operator selection and executor's compilation.

Identifying a Factory under Cost Criteria. Here, the user asks: "Which factory produces the module with the highest market price?" (Listing 4). GraphSeek decomposes this into three steps: compute the maximum market price, find the module with that price, and determine its production site. When an equality filter fails due to floating-point mismatch, the system automatically corrects itself by switching to an ordering-based query.

This example illustrates multi-step reasoning combined with automatic self-recovery from errors, as well as type reasoning and chaining of dependent tool calls. A human analyst would need to carefully manage numeric precision and manual joins; GraphSeek orchestrates all this seamlessly.
Comparison & Synthesis of Multiple Production Plans.

Next, the request is "Check unique production plans for three vehicle models whose names start with 'B', and tell me which one uses the largest number of modules. " (Listing 5). Here, GraphSeek can issue multiple tool calls concurrently and compare their outputs. The system performs fuzzy prefix matching, generates three separate calls to retrieve production plans, computes the number of distinct inputs per plan, and returns a synthesized comparison.

Without GraphSeek, such reasoning needs repeated queries, explicit name resolution, and manual aggregation across results. With tool modularity and executor-driven graph traversal, the LLM handles this as a single orchestration problem.

Self-Correction & Human-In-The-Loop. Finally, the request is a two-turn interaction: "What is the highest module cost?" followed by "Which module has this highest cost?" (Listing 6). The user first requests the highest module cost, and then asks which module has that cost. After obtaining max(unitCost), the agent initially tries an equality filter at rounded precision, receives an empty result, and then switches to a ranking-based operator (top-1 by unitCost) that is stable under floating-point representation. The executor deterministically runs the revised query, and the agent returns the correct module along with relevant attributes (tier, key), maintaining a clean semantic interaction while the underlying strategy adapts automatically. This use case showcases human-inthe-loop combined with automatic error recovery.

In traditional systems, such errors could confuse non-experts or require post-processing. GraphSeek shifts strategy automatically and shields the user from underlying data processing details. Simultaneously, it enables interactive analytics, so that the user can follow-up on requests, facilitating novel insights into the data.

div.head: EVALUATION
div.p: We now show advantages of GraphSeek over the state of the art in three relevant metrics: success rates of solved graph tasks, running times, and token costs. These three metrics capture the three orthogonal dimensions that determine whether an LLM-enhanced graph system is practical at scale: (1) task effectiveness, (2) systems efficiency, and (3) economic viability.

GraphSeek Variants. We test three variants of GraphSeek: a Basic GraphSeek (includes the Semantic Catalog and the Adaptive Toolset), a No Descriptors variant (no Semantic Catalog), and a Special Tools variant (with task-specific operators, manually designed for selected complex queries). The last variant showcases how GraphSeek supports extensibility through custom tool additions without altering the underlying framework.

Comparison Baselines. We consider LangChain [109], the most widely adopted open-source framework for LLM-enhanced data access and analytics. LangChain serves as a strong baseline because it offers flexible prompt engineering, tool calling, and integration with graph query engines such as Neo4j [153]. We consider three LangChain-based variants: Basic LangChain (it uses standard prompt templates that expose declarative schema structures but without any example instances), Enhanced LangChain that represents a best-effort optimization of LangChain prompts to maximize schema grounding and reduce ambiguity (it augments prompts with richly annotated schema information and includes synthetic example values), and Enhanced LangChain + Semantic Catalog.

Datasets. GraphSeek supports any labeled property graphs [25,27] or their variants such as heterogeneous graphs or knowledge graphs [26]. To showcase the usefulness of our design in real-world business settings, we harness an industry in-house graph with 230k vertices and 314k edges; we also use the WikiDataSets-Countries dataset [50] with 3.3k vertices and 10.2k edges.

Strict Token Budgets & Small LLMs. We use GPT-4o as the backend LLM (as opposed to heavier LLMs such as GPT-5) to keep reasoning accurate and affordable under strict token budgets; it also makes the system portable to other cost-efficient models (including small open models [13,60,91,199]) without redesign.
Additional evaluation setup details are in Appendix B.

div.head: Advantages for Industry Workloads
div.p: Success rate. Figure 4a shows that GraphSeek + Special Tools successfully answers all queries correctly, substantially outperforming LangChain variants and the structure-only ablation; Basic GraphSeek is generally second-best, with clear advantages over all LangChain baselines on harder queries. In contrast, Basic and Enhanced LangChain achieve low to moderate success and degrade markedly on multi-hop, schema-heavy tasks (queries 10-15). Even when equipped with iterative prompting, LangChain frequently fails to revise its approach: across retries it often emits essentially the same query repeatedly rather than reflecting on execution feedback and generating meaningfully different candidates, which directly contributes to its poor performance on harder queries.

Token usage. In token usage (Figure 4b) GraphSeek + Special Tools achieves consistently low and stable token costs across query families, and is often comparable to or lower than the LangChain baselines. In contrast, LangChain variants exhibit pronounced variance with occasional large spikes on specific queries, reflecting longer in-context traces and repeated attempts to recover from brittle generations. Basic GraphSeek remains competitive but shows slightly higher dispersion than the Special Tools variant, while GraphSeek -No Descriptors typically falls in between. Overall, GraphSeek improves cost-per-correct-answer on both axes: it reduces failures while keeping per-run token costs competitive, yielding cheaper correct outputs on realistic workloads.

Running time. In end-to-end latency, the best-performing configuration is GraphSeek + Special Tools, which consistently yields the fastest response times. After pruning redundant tools from the toolkit, the system becomes noticeably more responsive: median per-query latency drops to the low single-digit seconds (typically ≈2-3 s in our setup), and the agent remains interactive even when issuing multiple tool calls per query. Importantly, these additional steps do not translate into prohibitive delays. For instance, on queries Q3 and Q5 we observed cases where the agent initially

Basic LangChain Enhanced LangChain Enhanced LangChain + Semantic Catalog GraphSeek + Special Tools GraphSeek -No Descriptors Basic GraphSeek Q1 Q2 Q7 Q10 Q14 Q15 Q8 Q9 Q5 Q6 Q12 Q3 Q4 Q11 Q13 0/10 1/10 2/10 3/10 4/10 5/10 6/10 7/10 8/10 9/10 10/10 Success Rate Time-and Site-Sliced Production Queries Aggregation Multi-Hop Dependency Cost-Attribution & Process-Centric (a) Success rate. Q1 Q2 Q7 Q10 Q14 Q15 Q8 Q9 Q5 Q6 Q12 Q3 Q4 Q11 Q13 10 3 selected a suboptimal tool or provided imperfect arguments, but then corrected itself in subsequent steps and still produced the correct answer after a short sequence of 2-3 tool invocations. This indicates that the proposed design supports self-correction as a first-class behavior while maintaining interactive response times.

By contrast, the LangChain-based baseline exhibits dramatically longer runtimes than even Basic GraphSeek, consistent with orchestration overhead and the cost of iterative retries, typically needing 2 minutes per query on average, corresponding to a ∼40-60× slowdown, which makes LangChain unsuitable for interactive use.

Overall, these results suggest a clear design guideline: a small, carefully optimized set of general-purpose tools with compact descriptions can achieve both lower latency and higher reliability than a large, highly specialized toolkit. Beyond reducing invocation overhead, tool pruning also narrows the agent's action space, which appears to improve tool selection and parameterization, thereby reducing wasted calls and further stabilizing end-to-end runtimes.

div.head: Detailed Per-Query Analysis and Insights
div.p: When less context wins (Q1, Q8, Q9). A notable pattern appears on queries #1, #8, and #9. Excessive semantic detail occasionally over-constrains the agent: the semantic-heavy variants sometimes overuse available tools or fixate on narrower interpretations, increasing tokens without improving accuracy. For instance, on Q9 the GraphSeek -No Descriptors ablation study succeeds by leveraging the last-resort dynamic-Cypher tool directly. On the contrary, the semantic-rich variants persist with higher-level tools and incur overhead. A similar effect appears on Q8, where the semantic variant tries hard to route through specific tools. This suggests that more semantic context is not always better; it must be gated by query type and uncertainty.

The success on Q9 is enabled by a feature of GraphSeek in which it can (as a last resort, activated as an option after several failed attempts) completely bypass the compilation of the query from a pre-selected operator, and generate the Cypher query directly.

Semantics become essential (Q10-Q15). For queries 10-15, only models with semantic guidance solve the tasks reliably. These queries require disambiguating business entities, temporal qualifiers, and role-specific edges (e.g., linking processes to organizations in a specific year). Here, Semantic Descriptors significantly improve reasoning, lifting success to near ceiling for GraphSeek + Special Tools and a lot for Basic GraphSeek, while LangChain variants plateau at low success.

div.head: Ablation Takeaways
div.p: Impact of Semantic Catalog. Removing descriptors significantly reduces overall success, especially on disambiguation-heavy queries (Q10-Q15), but can occasionally help on simple list/lookup tasks by encouraging the agent to use the dynamic-Cypher tool directly (Q1, Q9). This motivates a fallback path where descriptors are switched off selectively if the task becomes sensitive to over-specification.

Value of special-purpose tools. The GraphSeek + Special Tools variant demonstrates that GraphSeek's architecture supports domain-tuned accelerators that both raise success and stabilize token usage. By collapsing multi-step plans into a single tool with targeted parameters, the agent avoids exploratory chains and redundant summaries, yielding consistent performance on hard queries.

div.head: Advantages in the WikiDataSets Benchmark
div.p: We evaluate the Countries benchmark of WikiDataSets [50], a standardized, topic-specific subgraph of Wikidata that preserves real-world schema heterogeneity (multiple administrative-unit types, aliases, and nested part-of /located-in relations) while remaining small enough for controlled, reproducible NL-to-graph analytics experiments (detailed in Appendix B).

The results are shown in Figure 5 (queries Q1-Q15 are detailed in Appendix C.2). GraphSeek attains near-ceiling success across essentially all queries, while LangChain-style baselines fail on many prompts and exhibit high variance across question types. The gains are most pronounced on queries that require mapping NL terms to the correct administrative type (e.g., province vs. state vs. county vs. federal district vs. constituent country) and then executing partof/located-in-whole reasoning reliably; these are exactly the failure modes of direct NL-to-query generation.

Failure mode analysis. We observe that prompt-only baselines often pick the wrong semantic target (treating the entity as a country rather than an administrative unit, or confusing the expected return type e.g., answering a type question with a location and vice versa. Second, they conflate near-synonyms and Wikidata-specific unit categories (e.g., mapping "federal district" to a "state"-like category, or mixing "province" and "region"). Third, they mishandle directionality in containment predicates (swapping part-of vs. has part, yielding inverted traversals). Finally, multi-hop chains prematurely terminate after a plausible intermediate node (returning the immediate parent rather than the requested enclosing political unit, especially when multiple containment paths exist).

Token usage. When using Basic GraphSeek, the token usage remains stable and comparatively low whenever the agent reliably grounds to the database runtime: the overall median token count is ≈29.7k tokens per query, with most successful runs clustering tightly around ≈29-31k tokens when the agent performs 2-4 database-backed tool calls. The main deviations are informative rather than noise: (i) unusually low token counts (e.g., ≈7k or ≈14k) typically correspond to cases where the LLM stopped consulting the database and instead relied on "insight"/prior knowledge after a failure or partial execution, which is precisely when correctness can degrade; and (ii) unusually high token counts (peaking at ≈55k) occur on harder queries that trigger longer corrective sequences, including occasional 6-call trajectories. Consistent with this interpretation, the descriptor-grounded Basic GraphSeek variant achieves a high aggregate success rate (with the few sub-ceiling cases concentrated in the most schema-ambiguous prompts, e.g., Q3 at 0.8 and Q4/Q8/Q9 at 0.9), while keeping the majority of successful runs in the same narrow token band.

Running time. Basic GraphSeek stays in the same interactive regime as discussed earlier: end-to-end latency remains on the order of a few seconds per query (typically ≈3-4 s in our setup), even when it executes multiple tool calls.

Taken together, these results reinforce the core design point: Semantic Descriptors help select the correct schema element/operator, while the Non-LLM runtime performs the expensive traversal and filtering deterministically; this combination yields both high accuracy and predictable, interactive cost/latency profiles compared to prompt-only baselines. GraphSeek also uses substantially fewer tokens per query: by delegating database-grade execution to a Non-LLM runtime and returning only compact summaries, it avoids long in-context intermediate traces and repeated re-querying observed in baselines, yielding lower token cost while improving accuracy.

div.head: RELATED WORK
div.p: Our work touches upon numerous domains. In Section 2, we have already described the relationship between GraphSeek and graph representation learning, general LLM-enhanced data analytics beyond graphs, and recent works sitting at the overlap of prompt engineering and graphs. We now discuss other related areas.

Graph databases. A mature ecosystem of graph databases supports property graphs and RDF/OWL, optimized for fast adjacency accesses and transactional workloads, which are capabilities that LLM-enhanced analytics can invoke rather than re-implement [5,6,27,67,81,90,106,107]. Representative systems include Neo4j [153], Graph Database Interface [24,27], TigerGraph [174,175], Amazon Neptune [3], JanusGraph [99], Virtuoso [141], ArangoDB [9], Re-disGraph [151], Dgraph [70], Stardog [168], and GraphDB [140], among many others [2,7,8,20,25,31,48,53,58,73,76,79,98,102,103,129,130,134,138,142,147,152,173,195,208,213]. In LLM-enhanced graph analytics, these engines serve as the execution substrate: the LLM plans, grounds user intent to schema, and compiles to Cypher/Gremlin/SPARQL-like queries; the DBMS then executes at scale with ACID/ACL semantics. Our approach follows this separation of concerns by delegating physical execution to a non-LLM executor while keeping prompts small and schema-aware.

Graph analytics frameworks and schemes. High-performance graph mining and analytics stacks provide scalable primitives (e.g., traversals, motif finding, community detection) and domain-specific methods that LLM agents can orchestrate. Examples include GraphMineSuite (GMS) [42], ProbGraph [36], and a large body of systems/algorithms spanning parallel traversal, frequent subgraph mining, dynamic/streaming updates, coloring, communication-avoiding kernels, and numerous others [15, 18, 22, 23, 29, 32, 34, 39, 43, 54, 63, 65, 72, 84-86, 94, 100, 137, 148, 157, 159, 167, 170, 192]. Concretely, such systems expose composable operators; our LLM layer can call such operators as tools, letting the backend handle execution details.

Agent systems. The landscape of agent systems has emerged during the last years, with example designs such as Reflexion [166], AutoGen [184], MetaGPT [93], Knowledge Graph of Thoughts [38], and many others [17,35,37,45,57,64,74,87,101,104,109,122,133,156,169,171,202,209,212]. These schemes have been applied to numerous tasks in reasoning [35,46,66], planning [96,146,165,179], software development [172], and others [44,160,188]. The difference to our work is that GraphSeek can be seen as the first agent system that offers LLM-enhanced interactive graph analytics.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). RAG connects LLM prompts to external stores (documents, tables, vector indexes, or graph repositories) to reduce hallucinations and compress context. Designs range from classic document RAG to adaptive and hierarchical retrieval [1,11,33,68,74,83,88,95,97,113,123,127,132,158,182,190,193,194,197,204]. For graph analytics, we use RAG to retrieve schema snippets and tool documentation, fetch compact graph summaries (neighborhood sketches, degree/label histograms), and incorporate prior conversation artifacts; all are injected as fixed-size features into prompts, preserving small-context compatibility.

Task decomposition & planning. Decomposition turns complex graph questions into executable subgoals (e.g., "identify candidates," "filter by constraints," "aggregate KPIs"). Methods such as ADaPT [146], ANPL [96], HuggingGPT [165], and graph-based control-flow encodings (ScrapeGraphAI [143], LangGraph [108]) provide templates for converting natural instructions into DAGs of tool invocations. Our system adopts iterative planning with schema/tool grounding, allowing safe backtracking: failed subgoals trigger verified re-plans rather than longer prompts.

Building and reusing past experience. Persistent memories and experience bases capture successful tool chains, schemas, and failure modes. RAP [101], ExpeL [202], ReGAL [169], Hypothesesto-Theories [211], KnowAgent [209], and Reflexion [166] demonstrate how episodic/task memories, action knowledge bases, and reflective critiques improve stability and sample efficiency. In our system, we use the past history of messages within a given task to tune the next data reasoning step.

Fine-tuning for graph analytics. Graph-specific instruction tuning and reinforcement learning (RL) can specialize LLMs for tool use and structural reasoning. GraphTool-Instruction [177] finetunes for decomposition and tool invocation on graph tasks, while GraphChain [180] applies reinforcement learning to learn toolchaining policies at scale. More broadly, these works exemplify parametric specialization for graph reasoning that needs training. In contrast, our scheme targets inference-time adaptivity with no finetuning, emphasizing plug-and-play deployment, token efficiency, and portability across small-context models.

Multi-agent collaboration. Coordination among multiple experts (planners, verifiers, explainers) can yield robustness and throughput. CodeAgent [172], AutoAgents [57], DyLAN [122], Professional Agents [64], MBTI-in-Thoughts [21], and AutoGen [184] study division of labor and communication protocols; Agent Forest [115] uses sampling/voting, while GPTSwarm [212] models agents as computational graphs. Although our current system achieves high fidelity with a single orchestrator, these strategies are complementary for future scaling (e.g., parallel exploration of alternative graph plans with cross-checking).

Tool use and adaptivity. Tool-augmented LLMs extend beyond text-only reasoning to call external programs and databases [44,160,188]. ToolNet [120] models multi-tool application as a directed graph to schedule iterative tool use at scale. Critically for evolving enterprise graphs, non-static tools that can be revised at inference time (e.g., DynaSaur [139]) enable the agent to synthesize/modify operators as schemas drift. Our system embraces this paradigm: we maintain a self-adaptive toolset that the LLM can refine (parameterize, compile, or swap) on demand, while confining prompts to constant-size schema/tool descriptors and delegating heavy computation to the executor.

div.head: CONCLUSION
div.p: We present GraphSeek, a framework for large-scale LLM-enhanced graph analytics that employs a principled separation between a Semantic Plane and an Execution Plane. In the Semantic Plane, the LLM agent plans and interprets analytics requests by reasoning over compact, execution-derived summaries and a Semantic Catalog that annotates both schema and available operators; in the Execution Plane, a deterministic Non-LLM Executor performs data access, filtering, and post-processing. Complementing this core design, GraphSeek supports an Adaptive Toolset whose operator implementations can evolve at inference time, ensuring robustness against evolving data at controlled (and often reduced) token budgets.

Looking forward, the Semantic Catalog + plane separation generalizes beyond property graphs: to tabular and relational databases, time-series pipelines, heterogeneous modalities (documents, logs, events), and streaming settings such as dynamic graphs where summaries must be incremental. Deeper integration with database engines (e.g., cost-aware compilation), principled policies for catalog construction, and learned budget controllers for operator selection are promising directions. We believe GraphSeek provides an operational foundation for scalable, trustworthy LLM-based analytics over evolving, industry-scale irregular datasets.

div.head: A SYSTEM DESIGN: ADDITIONAL DETAILS
div.p: We provide additional details on the system design.

div.head: A.1 Example Semantic Descriptors
div.p: We showcase example Semantic Descriptors and their associated schema elements in Figure 6.

div.head: Seman�c Descriptor
div.p: The resource is externally supplied.
Denotes that a component or subsystem, such as a ba�ery or drive assembly, is physically and func�onally incorporated into a higher-level product during assembly.

div.head: A.2 Task Lifecycle
div.p: The task lifecycle is pictured on the left side of Figure 7. Each user interaction in GraphSeek is encapsulated within a Task, which serves as a structured container for execution state, tool usage, and result traceability. Upon receiving a new user request 𝑥, the system instantiates a new Task object 1 . As the LLM Agent processes the request, every tool invocation is logged within the Task 2 , along with a reference to its corresponding result stored in the Hybrid Data Store 3 .

This design allows the Task to act as an execution trace: it records not only the sequence of tool calls but also links to intermediate and final outputs, including graphs, tables, or summaries. Once the LLM produces the final response, the Task is marked as completed and becomes immutable, ready for future auditions 4 .

div.head: A.3 Tool Adaptation Process
div.p: The tool adaptation is pictured on the right side of Figure 7. Graph-Seek supports dynamic tool adaptation via an inference-time, LLMdriven code synthesis loop. When a schema changes, the LLM Agent may inspect a tool (including its utility call chain) 1 -3 , propose a revised template or implementation consistent with the operator signature declared in the Semantic Catalog, and submit a candidate.

The LLM Agent then generates a new version of the tool 4 . This candidate is automatically validated against a strict schema (defined with JSON Schema or any other specification form) provided in the system prompt. If validation fails, the LLM Agent is tasked with re-generating the code in light of the returned error message 5 . Validated code is stored as an unverified version 6 and, after light- weight human approval, promoted to the live library 7 .

div.head: A.4 Example Compact Summary
div.p: An example compact 𝑂 (1) graph summary is shown in Listing 1. It depicts constant-size metadata for a subgraph centered on a single EV configuration. The fields summary, preview_graph_stats (aggregate statistics), and a fixed-size preview_graph together describe a local neighborhood around a vehicle model (e.g., Model Z3), capturing semantically typed nodes such as BatteryModule, Drive-Assembly, VehicleModel, FactorySite, and AssemblyLine, along with key relationships including INTEGRATED_IN, OUTPUTS, IN-STALLED_AT, and CONNECTED_TO. The full subgraph remains addressable via a graph_handle, enabling the LLM to reason over this compact preview while offloading deeper traversal or analytics to the Non-LLM Executor.

div.head: B EVALUATION SETUP: FURTHER DETAILS B.1 Models
div.p: All experiments use OpenAI's GPT-4o model. We enforce strict prompt budgets: a per-message cap of 20,000 tokens for any single user input (never reached in our workloads) and a conversation history budget of 10,000 tokens, after which older content is summarized or pruned as described in Section 4.

div.head: B.2 Datasets & Benchmarks
div.p: The underlying processing plant property graph slice contains 229,551 nodes, 313,635 relationships, and 20 distinct property keys, representative of an industry-scale workload for our queries and ablation studies.

The WikiDataSets-Countries dataset [50] and benchmark is a topic-specific knowledge-graph subgraph extracted from Wikidata, where entities are included if they are instances of country (Wikidata topic entity) or any subclass thereof (e.g., historical countries), yielding a labeled multirelational graph with facts of the form (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡). In addition to edges between selected nodes, the dataset retains facts from selected nodes to non-selected Wikidata entities as node attributes, enabling queries that mix inter-country relations (e.g., borders, diplomatic relations) with property-style information (e.g., currency, capital). The released artifact includes separate files for edges, attributes, and dictionaries mapping entity/relation IDs to human-readable labels, supporting reproducible loading and evaluation across systems. The published metadata for Countries reports 3,324 nodes and 10,198 edges, with 48 distinct relation types, plus 17,965 distinct attributes across 37,096 attribute facts (and 145 distinct attribute-relation types).

div.head: B.3 Metrics & Success Rate
div.p: Each repeat is a fresh run in which the LLM Agent may internally issue any number of tool calls or self-corrections; the default value we use is up to 10. For LangChain, similarly, each repeat consists of up to 10 attempts within a single conversation, where each attempt sees the full history. A repeat is considered successful if any attempt yields a correct final answer, i.e., the LLM asserts that its graph query is correct, the query executes successfully, and the resulting output allows the model to produce a response consistent with the ground truth. Numerical tasks require exact correctness; tasks 1 { 2 " summary " : " Assembly plan for VehicleModel ' Model Z3 ' ( key 'EV -Z3 ') at integration tier ' Tier -0 ' under cost class ' Actuals ' for calendar year 2023 ( Jan -Dec ) . 18 " relationships " : [ 19 { " id " : " e1 " , " type " : " INSTALLED_AT " , " source " : " n2 " , " target " : " n5 " } ,
{ " id " : " e2 " , " type " : " OUTPUTS " , " source " : " n2 " , " target " : " n1 " } ,
{ " id " : " e3 " , " type " : " INTEGRATED_IN " , " source " : " n3 " , " target " : " n2 " } ,

{ " id " : " e4 " , " type " : " CONNECTED_TO " , " source " : " n4 " , " target " : " n1 " } Q1 List all battery modules produced in a given calendar year. Q2 List all vehicle models assembled during a specified quarter of a given year.
Q7 List all battery modules produced at a specified factory site. Q10 For a given vehicle model identifier and month/year, list the factory sites scheduled to assemble it.
Q14 Enumerate all assembly processes executed at a specified factory site.
Q15 For a given vehicle model and quarter, list the assembly processes used to manufacture it.

Structurally, these are 1-2 hop traversals over a bipartite or tripartite subgraph linking product or process nodes to calendar and site nodes. The queries start from one of these axes (time, site, or vehicle) and fan out over a small star-shaped neighborhood to recover the matching nodes. From the engine's perspective, this family stresses efficient indexed access on attributes (e.g., year, quarter), expansion over low-diameter patterns, and simple projections back to domain entities (modules, models, processes) that a downstream analytics pipeline would consume.

C.1.2 Multi-Site and Multi-Period Neighborhood Aggregation. These queries introduce degree constraints and aggregation over distinct neighbors, moving beyond simple selection to counting structures in the graph.
Q8 Find vehicle models that are currently manufactured at three or more factory sites.
Q9 Find vehicle models scheduled for production in two or more distinct time periods.

In graph terms, they compute degree-based predicates over the vehicle-site and vehicle-time bipartite relations (e.g., deg site (vehicle) ≥ 3 or deg time (vehicle) ≥ 2). This requires grouping by vehicle nodes and aggregating over incident edges to distinct neighbors, which can be seen as a constrained neighborhood expansion followed by a group-by on the center node. Such queries are representative of capacity planning and risk-spreading analyses in EV manufacturing (e.g., "which models are geographically diversified"), while also stressing the graph engine's ability to perform combiners and degree computations efficiently at scale. Q5 Given a particular component module, determine which vehicle models would be impacted by a shortage.
Q6 If a given component module becomes unavailable, determine which factory sites and regions would be affected.

These are type-filtered selections possibly combined with a containment predicate. For Q5/Q9, the canonical form is {𝑣 : (𝑣, type, State)∧(𝑣, locatedIn, Australia)} (similarly for FederalDistrict and UnitedStates). Q13 can be seen as a schema-level query over the induced subgraph of England's administrative units: return the set (or a representative) of subdivision types 𝑐 such that ∃𝑣 (𝑣, locatedIn, England) ∧ (𝑣, type, 𝑐). From the engine's perspective, this family stresses multi-predicate filtering (type ∧ location), and for Q13, aggregation over types (distinct 𝑐). C.2.5 Political Composition / Multi-Answer Part Lists. These queries ask for a political unit and its other constituents, yielding a small set of entities.

Q12 England is a constituent of which political unit, along with which other parts? Graph-theoretically, Q12 composes (i) an upward containment step to find the parent political unit 𝑢 such that (England, partOf, 𝑢), and (ii) a sibling expansion to enumerate other constituents {𝑣 : (𝑣, partOf, 𝑢)}, typically with a filter 𝑣 ≠ England. This is a minimal example of a two-stage join: find a hub node 𝑢 and then fan out over its incident edges, stressing the system's ability to retain intermediate bindings (the found 𝑢) and reuse them across steps.

/ The LLM Agent first constructs an appropriate tool call signature : 5 get_nodes ( 6 node_type = " DriveAssembly " ,

The UI also renders the blueprint as a graph visualization . 31 // The LLM Agent replies to the user :32 The unique manufacturing blueprint for vehicle model EV-X7 includes: 33 * Main battery module: BM-A1 (primary energy source). 34 * A drive assembly: DA-B7, integrating BM-A1 and additional subsystems. 35 * One or more assembly lines and factory sites where the manufacturing stages occur.36 The blueprint graph captures, for each node, the associated production window and cost under the 'Plan' profile.Listing 3: Example use case of GraphSeek -Inspecting a manufacturing blueprint for a vehicle model.

note.p: To ensure anonymity of the in-house datasets, we fully change the naming and in-house data specifics from the original one into EV manufacturing; however, we keep the original general graph structure and make sure that all the insights and observations remain valid.

div.p: We thank Hussein Harake, Colin McMurtrie, Mark Klein, Angelo Mangili, and the whole CSCS team granting access to the Ault and Daint machines, and for their excellent technical support. We thank Timo Schneider for immense help with infrastructure at SPCL. We thank Katarzyna Zaczek and Tomasz Bogdał, for help with the project. This project received funding from the European Research Council (Project PSAP, No. 101002047), and the European High-Performance Computing Joint Undertaking (JU) under grant agreement No. 955513 (MAELSTROM). This project received funding from the European Union's HE research and innovation programme under the grant agreement No. 101070141 (Project GLACIATION).
where certain tuples must be fetched are deemed executed correctly if at least one of the needed tuples has been retrieved.

div.head: B.4 Workloads
div.p: We evaluate GraphSeek on a suite of 30 queries that exercise this model end-to-end, spanning period-based listings, multi-hop impact analysis (e.g., shortages), schema-aware entity/process linkages, and cross-modal presentation (tables/graphs). All queries are listed in Appendix C. For each query and method, we run 10 independent repeats with the same LLM backend and database.

div.head: C USED GRAPH QUERIES: DETAILS C.1 Industry Dataset
div.p: We evaluate on the following high-level graph queries (reformulated from the original domain to avoid disclosing collaborator-specific schema details):
Q1 List all battery modules produced in a given calendar year. Q2 List all vehicle models assembled during a specified quarter of a given year.
Q3 Identify vehicle models built using a specified assembly-process family (given by a human-readable label).
Q4 Report aggregated costs of auxiliary modules (e.g., scrap or side assemblies) at a specified assembly tier during a given year.
Q5 Given a particular component module, determine which vehicle models would be impacted by a shortage.
Q6 If a given component module becomes unavailable, determine which factory sites and regions would be affected.
Q7 List all battery modules produced at a specified factory site. Q8 Find vehicle models that are currently manufactured at three or more factory sites.
Q9 Find vehicle models scheduled for production in two or more distinct time periods.
Q10 For a given vehicle model identifier and month/year, list the factory sites scheduled to assemble it.
Q11 For a given vehicle model, identify component modules with the greatest contribution to total cost and/or output.
Q12 Determine which vehicle models are planned to include a specified component module.
Q13 Identify assembly processes associated with a specified business unit during a given year.
Q14 Enumerate all assembly processes executed at a specified factory site.

Q15 For a given vehicle model and quarter, list the assembly processes used to manufacture it. For analysis, we group the queries into four families that exercise different graph-structured access patterns and stress distinct aspects of the underlying graph engine (e.g., neighborhood expansion, multiway joins, and aggregation).

C.1.1 Time-and Site-Sliced Production Queries. These queries primarily perform attribute-filtered selection on nodes (e.g., vehicle models, battery modules, assembly processes), combined with short-range neighborhood traversal to calendar and factory-site Q12 Determine which vehicle models are planned to include a specified component module.

They correspond to reachability and constrained neighborhoodexpansion queries over two coupled subgraphs: the productstructure graph and the deployment graph (vehicles to sites/regions). Typical patterns here are 2-4 hop paths with branching (a component can appear in multiple assemblies, each used in multiple vehicles, deployed at multiple factories). This family captures "what-if" reasoning that may be difficult to express compactly in flat relational schemas but natural in a graph setting, and it stresses the ability of the system to perform multi-hop traversals with selective pruning and to propagate impact sets across large neighborhoods.
C.1.4 Cost Attribution and Process/Organization-Centric Views. These queries combine graph traversal with aggregation, ranking, and alternative "root" perspectives on the same underlying topology.
Q3 Identify vehicle models built using a specified assembly-process family (given by a human-readable label).
Q4 Report aggregated costs of auxiliary modules (e.g., scrap or side assemblies) at a specified assembly tier during a given year.
Q11 For a given vehicle model, identify component modules with the greatest contribution to total cost and/or output.
Q13 Identify assembly processes associated with a specified business unit during a given year.

div.head: C.2 WikiDataSets-Countries Benchmark
div.p: We evaluate on the following high-level graph queries: For analysis, we group the Countries queries into five families that exercise different graph-structured access patterns over an administrative-division KG (e.g., type constraints, part-whole joins, and attribute-based predicates such as hemisphere). Throughout, we view the dataset as a labeled multirelational graph with entity nodes and edges (facts) of the form (ℎ, 𝑟, 𝑡) plus attribute facts (𝑣, 𝑎, 𝛼), where 𝛼 is a literal (e.g., Northern Hemisphere).

div.head: C.2.1 Geospatial Attribute Predicates (Hemisphere). These queries test attribute-level reasoning about geographic properties.
div.p: Q2 In which hemisphere is Botswana located? Q3 Which country is located in the Northern Hemisphere?

Graph-theoretically, this family is dominated by attribute lookup and attribute filtering. Q2 is a single-node attribute read (Botswana, hemisphere, 𝛼), whereas Q3 is an inverse selection {𝑣 : (𝑣, hemisphere, Northern Hemisphere)} possibly followed by a type constraint 𝑣 ∈ Country. From the engine's perspective, these queries stress efficient access to literal-valued properties and simple selection predicates, rather than deep traversal. C.2.2 Containment / Located-In Joins. These queries ask for the containing political unit of a named place.

Q1 Where is the state of Morelos located? Q8 Where is California located? Q10 In which country is Yukon located? Q11 Cornwall is a county located in which country? Q14 Heilongjiang is a province located in which country?

Structurally, these are short-path part-whole queries, typically a 1-hop join along a containment predicate such as locatedIn / partOf. In abstract form, they compute 𝑡 such that (𝑣, locatedIn, 𝑡) (or (𝑣, partOf, 𝑡)) for a given 𝑣. Depending on the KG schema, 𝑡 may be a country or an intermediate administrative unit, yielding a small number of additional hops (e.g., 𝑣 → State → Country). This family stresses reliable relation selection (choosing the correct containment edge among many plausible predicates) and robust entity linking for administrative units.

div.head: C.2.3
div.p: Administrative-Type Identification. These queries ask for the administrative type of a named entity (e.g., "province", "state").
Q4 What type of area is Mexico City in Mexico? Q6 New South Wales is an Australian what? Q7 What type of subdivision is Alabama in the United States of America?
Q15 Which type of subdivision is Xinjiang in the People's Republic of China?

Graph-wise, these correspond to type edges (e.g., instanceOf, subclassOf) plus optional conditioning on a containing unit. In abstract form, they recover a class 𝑐 such that (𝑣, type, 𝑐), sometimes with an added constraint that 𝑣 is contained in a specified parent 𝑝 (e.g., (𝑣, locatedIn, 𝑝)). This family stresses correct disambiguation between entity names and type labels, and (when present) joining type reasoning with containment.

div.head: C.2.4
div.p: Selection Under Type and Location Constraints. These queries invert the previous family: given a class (and sometimes a country), return an example entity.

Q5 Which one is a state in Australia? Q9 Which federal district is located in the United States? Q13 Which type of subdivision is found in England? filters = [{ " key " : " unitCost " , " operator " : " = " , " value " : 99.42 , " value_type " : " number " }] ,

User input request : 2 At which factory site is the component with the highest market price manufactured? 3
LLM Agent first constructs an appropriate tool call signature : 5 get_nodes ( 6 node_type = " BatteryModule
The Non -LLM Executor generates and executes a Cypher query
MATCH ( b : BatteryModule )
The LLM Agent then realizes that the data does not identify the component itself . Thus , it generates another appropriate tool call signature : 15 get_nodes ( node_type = " BatteryModule

The Non -LLM Executor generates and executes a Cypher query : 18 MATCH ( b : BatteryModule ) RETURN b ORDER BY b . marketPrice DESC LIMIT 1 19 20 // The tool execution returns the following example data : 21 { 22
27 28 // The LLM Agent then generates a final appropriate tool call signature : 29 get_factory_sites_for_module ( module_name = " BM -9003
LLM Executor generates and executes a Cypher query : 32 MATCH
